title: Big-O You Can Feel: Complexity Through Re-renders and DOM Work
description: Algorithms for frontend engineers — taught entirely through the costs you actually pay in the browser, so you *feel* asymptotic complexity instead of memorizing it. Three connected arcs build the intuition: reading Big-O straight off real render code (a `.find()` inside a `.map()`, `querySelectorAll` in a loop, layout thrashing from interleaved reads and writes, and the best, worst, and amortized cases plus the constant factors that bite the main thread); why `array.includes()` inside a filter quietly turns a list quadratic and how a `Set` or `Map` collapses the same lookup to `O(1)`; and the binary search hiding in plain sight behind virtualized lists, sorted inserts, and lower-/upper-bound queries. Aimed at frontend developers who want to connect classic algorithms and data structures to the React, DOM, and rendering work they already do every day — and finally read complexity off code instead of textbook pseudocode.
author: Quizbun

questions.title: This list render runs one `.find()` for every row it `.map()`s. It feels instant with 10 rows but freezes at 10,000. Why?

options.text: The work grows with *n*², so 1,000× more rows is about 1,000,000× more work.
JavaScript engines cap arrays near 10,000 elements and start dropping work.
React turns off batching past a few thousand nodes, so each row re-renders alone.
Garbage collection runs once per row, and 10,000 collections is the real cost.

questions.explanation: `find` is a linear scan, and you run it once per row, so the render does up to *n* × *n* = *n*² comparisons — a nested loop hiding behind two innocent-looking method calls. At *n* = 10 that is ~100 operations (invisible); at *n* = 10,000 it is ~100,000,000, which is hundreds of milliseconds of main-thread work that blocks paint and input. The intuition to internalize: when *n* grows 1,000×, *n*² grows 1,000× squared = 1,000,000×. The fix is to index once — build a `Map` of users by id (O(*n*)) and look each up in O(1) — turning the whole render back into O(*n*). The other options are myths: arrays are not capped at 10k, React keeps batching, and GC is not per-row.

questions.title: Why is this loop slow even though it touches each element only once?

options.text: Each read after a write forces a synchronous re-layout, so the browser recomputes layout *n* times instead of once.
Reading `offsetWidth` allocates a fresh layout object every iteration, leaking memory.
Building the `${...}` string is O(*n*) per iteration, making the loop O(*n*²).
`for...of` is much slower than an indexed `for` loop, and that overhead dominates.

questions.explanation: This is *layout thrashing* (forced synchronous reflow). Writing `style.width` invalidates the current layout; the very next iteration reads `offsetWidth`, which the browser can only answer with up-to-date geometry, so it must flush layout immediately. The read → write → read → write interleaving defeats the browser's natural batching and forces *n* separate layouts instead of one. Note the trap: the syntactic suspects (string building, `for...of`) are red herrings — the cost is structural. Fix it by batching: read every `offsetWidth` into an array first, then perform all the writes, so layout flushes a single time.

questions.title: What is the running time of calling `document.querySelectorAll('.cell')` once per row?

options.text: About O(*n*²): each call re-scans the whole document, and you make one call per row.
O(*n*): `querySelectorAll` is native, so the call itself counts as one cheap operation.
O(1): the browser caches selector results and returns the same `NodeList` instantly.
O(log *n*): the engine indexes the DOM by class name like a database.

questions.explanation: `querySelectorAll` walks the DOM to collect matches, so one call is O(size of the document), not O(1) — *native does not mean free*, it just means the loop is written in C++. Calling it inside a per-row loop makes it O(*n*) calls × O(*n*) scan each ≈ O(*n*²), and it allocates a brand-new `NodeList` every time (there is no caching). Hoist the query out of the loop so it runs once (O(*n*) total), or better, address the specific node you need. This is the archetypal 'looks simple, is expensive' line: one short statement that secretly re-reads the entire DOM on every pass.

questions.title: For `array.find(predicate)` on an unsorted array of *n* items, which best/worst-case description is correct?
questions.description: Think about where in the array the matching element is allowed to sit.

options.text: Best case O(1) when the match is first; worst case O(*n*) when it is last or absent.
Best and worst case are both O(*n*), because `find` always scans the entire array.
Best case O(log *n*) because the engine binary-searches; worst case O(*n*).
Best case O(*n*); worst case O(*n*²) when several elements match the predicate.

questions.explanation: `find` returns at the first match, so if the target is at index 0 it stops after one comparison — best case O(1). If the match is last or missing it scans all *n* — worst case O(*n*). It cannot binary-search (the array is not known to be sorted), and stopping at the first match means extra matches never make it quadratic. Why this matters for what you *feel*: a demo where the hit is always near the front hides an O(*n*) worst case that surfaces in production with cold or absent data. Best, worst, and average are three different questions — and the average is the one your users usually experience.

questions.title: You `push` *n* items onto a JavaScript array one at a time. What is the amortized cost per `push`?

options.text: Amortized O(1) per push: occasional resizes cost O(*n*), but they are rare enough to average out.
O(*n*) per push, because the array may copy all existing elements on every call.
O(log *n*) per push, because the backing capacity grows by doubling.
O(*n*²) total, because each push shifts every earlier element over by one.

questions.explanation: Growable arrays keep spare capacity and double it when full. Most pushes drop into spare space in O(1); occasionally one triggers an O(*n*) copy into a larger buffer. Because capacity doubles, those copies happen at sizes 1, 2, 4, 8, … and the total copy work across *n* pushes sums to roughly 2*n* — so the *amortized* cost is O(1) per push and O(*n*) for the whole batch. 'Amortized' means averaged over a sequence, not 'always': a single push can still spike, which is a genuine source of the occasional dropped frame. Contrast `unshift`/front-splice, which is O(*n*) *every* time because it shifts everything. Amortized-vs-worst-case is what tells you whether that one slow frame is expected or a bug.

questions.title: Two functions are both O(*n*) over the same list. Why might one still cause visible jank while the other does not?

options.text: Big-O hides constant factors; the janky one does heavy per-item work (layout, JSON parsing, node creation) on the main thread, so its large constant blows the frame budget.
One of them must secretly be O(*n*²); equal Big-O is impossible if their speeds differ.
The faster one is moved to the GPU automatically because it is also O(*n*).
O(*n*) always finishes within one 16 ms frame, so any jank means the code has a bug.

questions.explanation: Big-O describes how cost *grows* with *n*, not the size of each step. Two O(*n*) loops can differ by 100× in wall-clock time if one does a cheap arithmetic op per item and the other forces a reflow, parses JSON, or creates DOM nodes. The main thread has a hard budget — roughly 16 ms to hold 60 fps — shared with layout, paint, and input handling, so a big constant factor can miss that budget even at a modest *n*. 'Same Big-O' is necessary but not sufficient for 'feels the same', which is exactly why measured profiling complements asymptotic reasoning. Neither automatic GPU offload nor a 16 ms guarantee is real.

questions.title: Select every snippet whose running time is O(*n*²). Assume each array has length *n*.

options.text: `a.filter((x) => b.includes(x))`
`a.map((x) => x * 2).filter((x) => x > 0)`
`for (const x of a) for (const y of a) compare(x, y);`
`new Set(a).has(target)`
`a.reduce((sum, x) => sum + x, 0)`

questions.explanation: Quadratic appears when an O(*n*) operation runs *inside* another O(*n*) loop. `a.filter((x) => b.includes(x))` does an O(*n*) `includes` scan for each of *n* items → O(*n*²); the nested `for...of` is the textbook form of the same shape. The traps: chaining `.map().filter()` *looks* like more work but it is two separate linear passes — O(*n*) + O(*n*) = O(*n*); `new Set(a)` then one `.has` is O(*n*) to build plus O(1) to look up; and `reduce` is a single pass. The rule of thumb — sequential passes **add**, nested passes **multiply** — and method calls like `.includes`/`.indexOf`/`.find` hide a loop you must count.

questions.title: Which statements about estimating complexity from real frontend code are true? Select all that apply.

options.text: A call like `array.includes(x)` hides a loop, so nested inside another loop it contributes a factor of *n*, not O(1).
Updating state with `setItems([...items, next])` copies the whole array, so doing it in a loop of *n* updates is O(*n*²) total.
Chaining `.map().filter().map()` multiplies the passes, making it O(*n*³).
A `Map` or object lookup by key is roughly O(1), while scanning an array for the same key is O(*n*).
Constant factors can be ignored entirely, because Big-O drops them anyway.

questions.explanation: Reading complexity off real code is mostly about spotting hidden loops. `.includes`/`.find`/`.indexOf` are O(*n*) scans, so nesting them multiplies. Immutable updates like `[...items, next]` copy O(*n*) each time; repeating that for *n* updates is O(*n*²) total — a very common React performance bug (prefer one batched update, or a keyed `Map`). Key lookup in a `Map`/object is ~O(1) versus O(*n*) for an array scan, usually the biggest easy win. The two false statements are the headline misconceptions: chaining passes **adds** (O(*n*) + O(*n*) = O(*n*)), it does not multiply to O(*n*³); and although Big-O *mathematically* drops constant factors, on a 16 ms main-thread budget those constants are precisely what you feel.

questions.title: Read the snippet and type its overall time-complexity class, for example `O(n)`.
questions.description:
```js
function topThree(scores) {
  return scores
    .slice()              // copy the array
    .sort((a, b) => b - a)
    .slice(0, 3);         // keep the top 3
}
```

`scores` has length *n*. Give the dominant Big-O class.
questions.explanation: The dominant cost is `.sort`, a comparison sort at O(*n* log *n*). The leading `.slice()` copies the array in O(*n*) and the final `.slice(0, 3)` is O(1), so both are dominated and the function is **O(*n* log *n*)**. The trap is `.slice(0, 3)`: 'I only want the top 3' *feels* cheap, but you still sorted all *n* items to get them. If you genuinely needed only the top three, a single O(*n*) pass tracking three running maxima would beat sorting. Dropping lower-order terms — O(*n*) + O(*n* log *n*) + O(1) → O(*n* log *n*) — is exactly how you read a class off real code.

questions.title: Read the snippet and type its worst-case time-complexity class, for example `O(n)`.
questions.explanation: `filter` walks all *n* items of `a`, and for each one `includes` scans up to *n* items of `b`: *n* items × an O(*n*) scan = **O(*n*²)** (strictly O(*n*·*m*); they are equal when both arrays grow together). The single-line, declarative style hides the nested loop — the classic 'looks simple, is quadratic' trap. The fix is to put `b` in a `Set` once (O(*n*)) and test membership in O(1): `const set = new Set(b); return a.filter((x) => set.has(x));`, which makes the whole thing O(*n*). Whenever you see `.includes`/`.indexOf`/`.find` inside `.filter`/`.map`/a loop, suspect quadratic.

questions.title: What is the average time complexity of `arr.includes(x)` for an array of *n* elements?

options.text: `O(n)` — it scans element by element until it finds a match or reaches the end
`O(1)` — arrays hash their values for instant lookup
`O(log n)` — it binary-searches the array
`O(n log n)` — it sorts the array, then searches

questions.explanation: `Array.prototype.includes` walks the array from the front and compares each element, so an array of *n* items costs up to *n* comparisons — linear, `O(n)`. Arrays are neither hashed nor assumed to be sorted, so there is no `O(1)` or `O(log n)` shortcut. This one fact seeds every *the list got slow* bug in this quiz: a single linear scan is cheap, but repeating it inside a loop is not.

questions.title: A table renders *n* rows and stores the selected ids in an array `selectedIds` of length *m*. What is the cost of `rows.filter(r => selectedIds.includes(r.id))`?

options.text: `O(n · m)` — for each of *n* rows, `includes` scans up to *m* ids
`O(n + m)` — the two lengths add, they do not multiply
`O(n)` — `filter` visits each row only once
`O(m)` — only the number of selected ids matters

questions.explanation: `filter` visits each of the *n* rows once, but **inside** the callback `includes` runs its own `O(m)` scan of `selectedIds`. Nesting a linear scan inside a linear pass multiplies the costs into `O(n · m)`. With a few hundred rows and a few hundred selections that is tens of thousands of comparisons on **every** render — the classic quadratic blow-up behind a sluggish table. The cure, in the questions that follow, is to make the inner lookup `O(1)`.

questions.title: What is the average time complexity of `set.has(x)` for a `Set`?

options.text: `O(1)` — a `Set` is hash-based, so membership is constant time on average
`O(n)` — it scans its values just like an array
`O(log n)` — it keeps its values in a balanced tree
It depends on the order the values were inserted

questions.explanation: `Set` (like `Map`) is backed by a hash table: `has` hashes the value and jumps close to its slot instead of scanning. The ECMAScript specification even **requires** access times to be *sublinear on average* — effectively `O(1)`. Replacing `selectedIds.includes(id)` with `selectedSet.has(id)` is exactly what collapses the previous question's `O(n · m)` down to `O(n)`.

questions.title: Which UI tasks get faster or simpler when backed by a `Set` instead of an array?
questions.description: Select every task where a `Set` is the better fit.

options.text: Checking `isSelected(id)` for every row while rendering a large list
Removing duplicate values from a list of tags before display
Repeated membership tests such as *have we already seen this id?*
Reading an item by position, like *the 3rd row*, through an index

questions.explanation: A `Set` shines whenever the question is **is this value present?** or **are these values unique?** — both run in `O(1)` average time (`has`, plus automatic de-duplication on `add`). Membership checks inside a render loop and de-duping tags are textbook cases. What a `Set` is **not** for is positional access: it has no indices, so *give me the 3rd item* still needs an array (`[...set][2]` rebuilds one). Match the structure to the access pattern.

questions.title: By what rule does a `Set` decide that two values are the same, and therefore duplicates?

options.text: SameValueZero — like `===`, except that `NaN` equals `NaN`
Strict `===` exactly, so `NaN` is never equal to `NaN`
Loose `==`, applying type coercion
Reference identity, so equal primitives could be stored twice

questions.explanation: `Set` and `Map` compare values with **SameValueZero**: identical to `===` for almost everything, with one twist — `NaN` counts as equal to `NaN` (so a `Set` holds at most one `NaN`), and `+0` and `-0` are treated as the same value. It performs no `==` coercion, so `1` and `'1'` stay distinct. That is why `new Set([NaN, NaN]).size` is `1`, even though `NaN === NaN` evaluates to `false`.

questions.title: Which expression removes duplicate primitives from `arr` in `O(n)` average time?

options.text: `[...new Set(arr)]`
`arr.filter((x, i) => arr.indexOf(x) === i)`
`arr.sort().filter(Boolean)`
`Object.keys(arr)`

questions.explanation: Spreading an array through a `Set` and back — `[...new Set(arr)]` — de-duplicates in a single `O(n)` pass, because each insertion and membership check is `O(1)` on average. The `indexOf` one-liner also removes duplicates, but it runs an `O(n)` scan for **every** element, so it is `O(n²)` — the same quadratic trap as the table filter. `arr.sort().filter(Boolean)` mutates the original, merely groups equal values next to each other instead of removing them, and drops every falsy value; `Object.keys(arr)` returns the index strings `'0', '1', …`, not the values.

questions.title: Which are genuine advantages of a `Map` over a plain `{}` object for an `id → entity` lookup table?

options.text: `Map` keys may be any value — numbers, objects, even functions — with no coercion to strings
`Map` preserves true insertion order for every key; a plain object emits integer-like keys in numeric order first
`Map` exposes `.size` directly, whereas an object needs `Object.keys(obj).length`
`Map` lookups are `O(log n)` while object property reads are `O(n)`

questions.explanation: The first three are real, testable differences. (1) Object keys are coerced to strings or symbols, so `obj[1]` and `obj['1']` collide, while a `Map` keeps `1` and `'1'` as two distinct keys. (2) A plain object emits integer-like keys in **ascending numeric order** ahead of string keys, so `{ 2: 'a', 1: 'b' }` iterates as `1, 2`; a `Map` iterates in the exact order you inserted. (3) `map.size` is `O(1)`, while counting object keys allocates a throwaway array. The false statement is the complexity claim: **both** structures read in `O(1)` on average, so reaching for a `Map` is about key flexibility and ordering, not asymptotic speed.

questions.title: Using a plain `{}` as a string-keyed dictionary has footguns that a `Map` avoids. Which statements are true?

options.text: Assigning the key `'__proto__'` can change the object's prototype instead of storing an ordinary entry
`if (dict[key])` can be truthy for keys like `'toString'` that were never added, because they are inherited from `Object.prototype`
`Object.create(null)` makes a prototype-less object that sidesteps the inherited-key problem
A plain object keeps numeric keys as numbers, so `dict[1]` and `dict['1']` are different entries

questions.explanation: The first three are real reasons to prefer a `Map` (or `Object.create(null)`) for untrusted keys. Writing `dict['__proto__'] = {...}` can trigger the inherited `__proto__` setter and **reassign the prototype** — the root of prototype-pollution bugs — whereas `map.set('__proto__', v)` just stores a normal entry. And because every `{}` inherits from `Object.prototype`, keys like `toString` and `hasOwnProperty` *appear* to already exist, while a fresh `new Map().has('toString')` is `false`. The false statement is backwards: object keys are **coerced to strings**, so `dict[1]` and `dict['1']` are the *same* entry.

questions.title: You hold an array of *n* entities and must look many of them up by `id`. Which statements are correct?

options.text: Building a `Map` of `id → entity` once costs `O(n)`, after which each `map.get(id)` is `O(1)` on average
Calling `entities.find(e => e.id === target)` again and again costs `O(n)` per lookup
If the ids are numbers, a `Map` keyed by them preserves the number type, whereas a plain object would stringify each key
Once the lookup `Map` is built, each lookup degrades to `O(log n)`

questions.explanation: Pre-indexing is the antidote to repeated `find`. `entities.find(...)` re-scans from the top on every call — `O(n)` each — so *k* lookups cost `O(n · k)`. Build the index once with `new Map(entities.map(e => [e.id, e]))` (`O(n)`), and every later `map.get(id)` is `O(1)` on average, turning *k* lookups into `O(n + k)`. A `Map` also keeps the id's type intact (a number stays a number), unlike an object that stringifies its keys. The false option is, once more, the complexity claim: hash lookups are `O(1)` on average, not `O(log n)`.

questions.title: In the worst case, how many element comparisons does `includes` make in total? Enter the number.
questions.description: A list of **1,000** rows is filtered with `rows.filter(r => selectedIds.includes(r.id))`. The array `selectedIds` also holds **1,000** ids, and none of the rows match.
questions.explanation: Because no row matches, every `includes` call scans all the way to the end of `selectedIds` — 1,000 comparisons per row. Across 1,000 rows that is 1,000 × 1,000 = **1,000,000** comparisons for a *single* render. Build a `Set` once (`const sel = new Set(selectedIds)`) and each check becomes `O(1)`, cutting the work to about 1,000 operations — a thousandfold reduction. That gap is the whole *why did the table get slow* story: a missing hash structure quietly turned linear work quadratic.

questions.title: A teammate runs a binary-search helper on an array that isn't actually sorted. The value *is* present. What's the most likely result?

options.text: It can report 'not found' (or the wrong index): each step discards half the array based on an ordering that doesn't hold, and it fails silently with no error.
It throws a `RangeError`, because the sorted-input invariant is checked before the search starts.
It still finds the value, just in `O(n)` instead of `O(log n)`.
It finds the value but returns the index of a neighbouring duplicate instead.

questions.explanation: Binary search's *correctness* — not merely its speed — rests on the sorted precondition. Every step makes a deduction like "`arr[mid] < target`, so the target can only be to the right." On unsorted data that deduction is false, so the search can throw away the very half that holds the value and return a wrong index or 'not found'. Nothing checks the precondition for you: verifying sortedness is `O(n)`, which would defeat the whole point of an `O(log n)` search, so the failure is *silent* — the nastiest kind. The headline gotcha: binary search trades a precondition (sorted by the key you compare on) for speed, and honouring that precondition is the caller's job.

questions.title: With inclusive bounds (`hi = arr.length - 1`), which loop condition correctly searches for an exact match?

options.text: `lo <= hi` — when `lo === hi` the range still holds one unchecked element.
`lo < hi` — once `lo === hi` the element has already been found.
`lo !== hi` — stop the moment the two pointers meet.
`arr[mid] !== target` — loop until the midpoint equals the target.

questions.explanation: With an *inclusive* upper bound, the live range `[lo, hi]` contains both ends, so when `lo === hi` exactly one candidate remains and you must still check it — the condition has to be `lo <= hi`. Writing `lo < hi` exits one iteration early and misses any match that sits alone in a width-1 range: the textbook off-by-one. `arr[mid] !== target` feels intuitive but loops forever when the value is absent, because `mid` never lands on it. The clean rule is to pair conventions: *inclusive* `hi = length - 1` goes with `<=`; *exclusive* `hi = length` goes with `<`. Mixing the two halves is exactly where boundary bugs breed.

questions.title: Filling the blank, why is `lo = mid + 1` correct while `lo = mid` is a bug?

options.text: `mid` was just checked and ruled out, so the next range starts at `mid + 1`. `lo = mid` can loop forever: when `hi - lo === 1`, `mid` floors to `lo` and the range never shrinks.
`lo = mid` is the safe choice; the `+ 1` can step past the target and miss it.
Either works; `Math.floor` already guarantees the range shrinks every iteration.
You should write `hi = mid - 1` in the blank instead, to move the bound furthest from `mid`.

questions.explanation: Because `arr[mid]` was already compared and isn't the target, it must be excluded from the next range — the right half therefore starts at `mid + 1` (and, symmetrically, the left half ends at `mid - 1`). Drop the `+ 1` and the loop can stall: with `lo = 0, hi = 1`, `mid = Math.floor(0.5) = 0`; if `arr[0] < target` you set `lo = 0` again — same range, forever. Excluding `mid` is precisely what makes the range *strictly* shrink each pass (the loop's progress guarantee), which complements the invariant "the target, if present, always lies in `[lo, hi]`." Floor biases `mid` toward `lo`, so it's the `lo` side that needs the `+ 1` to escape.

questions.title: What does a *lower-bound* binary search return for a target `x` in a sorted array?

options.text: The index of the first element `>= x` — equivalently, the count of elements strictly less than `x`, and the slot where `x` would be inserted before any equal values.
The index of the first element strictly `> x`.
The index where `arr[mid] === x`, or `-1` when `x` is absent.
The index of the last element `<= x`.

questions.explanation: Lower-bound answers "where does `x` *begin*?" — the first slot whose value is not less than `x`. If `x` is present it lands on the first copy; if absent it lands on the gap where `x` belongs, which is why it doubles as an insertion point. Its sibling, upper-bound, returns the first element strictly *greater* than `x` (one past the last copy). The two coincide when `x` is absent and straddle the run of duplicates when it's present. The exact-match search taught first in school (return the index or `-1`) is actually the *less* common need in real code: most 'binary search in disguise' tasks — insertion index, first event at/after a time, first visible row — are lower-/upper-bound queries.

questions.title: `react-window` and `@tanstack/virtual` render only the visible rows. With *variable* row heights, how do they find the first visible row for a given `scrollTop`?

options.text: Binary-search the cumulative `offsets` array for `scrollTop`: the last row whose top is `<= scrollTop` is the first visible one — `O(log n)` per scroll.
Walk from row 0, adding each height until the running total passes `scrollTop` — `O(n)` per scroll event.
Divide `scrollTop` by a fixed row height to get the index directly in `O(1)`.
Keep a hash map from every `scrollTop` value to a row index for `O(1)` lookups.

questions.explanation: Fixed-height rows let you divide `scrollTop` by the row height for an `O(1)` index — but variable heights break that, so the libraries keep a sorted array of cumulative offsets (a prefix sum of measured heights) and binary-search it. The first visible row is the last one whose top offset is `<= scrollTop` — i.e. the upper-bound of `scrollTop`, minus one. This is the literal "binary search in disguise": nobody calls it binary search, but running an `O(n)` scan on every scroll or `requestAnimationFrame` frame would jank a long list, whereas `O(log n)` stays smooth at 100k rows. `react-window`'s `VariableSizeList` and `@tanstack/virtual` both ship exactly this search over their measurement cache.

questions.title: You keep an array sorted by inserting each new item at the position a binary search finds, via `splice`. What's the cost of one insertion, and what dominates?

options.text: `O(n)`: binary search finds the slot in `O(log n)`, but `splice` must shift up to `n` later elements to make room, and that shift dominates.
`O(log n)`: the binary search makes the whole insertion logarithmic.
`O(1)`: knowing the array is already sorted lets you drop the element straight in.
`O(n log n)`: the array is re-sorted after every insertion.

questions.explanation: This is the most common over-claim about binary search: it accelerates the *find*, not the *insert*. Locating the index is `O(log n)`, but inserting into the middle of a contiguous array shifts every later element over by one — `O(n)` — so a single insert is `O(n)`, and building a sorted array by repeated insertion is `O(n²)`. Binary search still earns its keep: comparisons are often costlier than the raw memory move, and many tasks only need the *index* ("where would this timestamp go?") without mutating anything. But if you need genuinely cheap ordered inserts, reach for a different structure — a balanced tree or skip list. Knowing *where* is fast; *making room* is not.

questions.title: Select every task that is really a binary search over sorted data, even if it's never called that.

options.text: Finding the first visible row in a variable-height virtualized list from `scrollTop`.
Choosing where a new timestamp belongs in a sorted event log.
Inserting a value into an already-sorted array without re-sorting it.
Looking up a user object by `id` in a `Map`.
Finding the largest number in an unsorted array.

questions.explanation: The three correct items share the tell-tale signature: a *sorted* sequence plus a question of the form "which position does this fall at?" — the first row past a pixel offset, the insertion slot for a timestamp, the insertion slot for a value. All are lower-/upper-bound searches running in `O(log n)`. The two distractors fail the precondition test: a `Map` lookup is a hash probe (`O(1)`, order-independent — there's no order to bisect), and finding a max in unsorted data is an unavoidable `O(n)` scan, since with no sorted order there's no half you can safely discard. The reusable instinct: *sorted data + "which position?" ⇒ reach for binary search.*

questions.title: Which statements about binary search are true? Select all that apply.

options.text: It needs the data sorted by the key you compare on; on unsorted input it can miss an element that's actually present.
Lower-bound and upper-bound return the same index when the target is absent and differ by exactly the number of equal elements when it's present.
Doubling the array length adds only one comparison to the worst case, because the work grows like `log2(n)`.
Because it's `O(log n)`, binary search is faster than a `Map`'s `O(1)` lookup for finding a single key.
In JavaScript, `Math.floor((lo + hi) / 2)` can overflow to a negative `mid` for arrays of a few million items, so you must write `lo + Math.floor((hi - lo) / 2)`.

questions.explanation: True: the sorted precondition is about *correctness*, not just speed; lower- and upper-bound coincide when the value is absent and bracket its run of duplicates when present (their gap *is* the multiplicity); and logarithmic growth means each doubling of `n` adds a single step (1M → 20, 2M → 21). False: `O(log n)` does not beat `O(1)` — a hash `Map` wins for single-key lookup, and binary search earns its place only when you need the *ordering* a hash can't give (ranges, neighbours, insertion points). The overflow warning is genuine in fixed-width-integer languages (Java/C `int`), but JavaScript numbers are 64-bit floats, exact up to 2^53, so `(lo + hi)` for a few million is nowhere near overflowing; `lo + (hi - lo) / 2` is fine defensive style but is *not required* in JS.

questions.title: Worst case, how many comparisons (halving steps) does binary search make on a sorted array of `n = 1,000,000` items? Enter a whole number.
questions.description: Each step compares the target with the middle element and discards half of what's left.
questions.explanation: Each comparison halves the remaining range, so the worst case is how many times you can halve 1,000,000 before nothing is left: `ceil(log2(1,000,000)) = 20`, because `2^19 = 524,288 < 1,000,000 <= 1,048,576 = 2^20`. Put another way, 20 yes/no questions are enough to single out one item among ~1,000,000. Contrast a linear scan's worst case of 1,000,000 comparisons on the same data — roughly 50,000× more work. That gap, 20 versus a million, is exactly why the 'in disguise' use cases (virtual lists, sorted logs, sorted inserts) go to the trouble of keeping their data sorted.

questions.title: The list grows from 1,000,000 to 2,000,000 items. How many *additional* worst-case comparisons does binary search now need? Enter a whole number.
questions.description: Binary search's worst case grows like `log2(n)` — so think about what a single doubling of `n` does to it.
questions.explanation: Just one. The worst case is `ceil(log2(n))`: at 1,000,000 it's 20, and at 2,000,000 it's 21, because `2^20 = 1,048,576 < 2,000,000 <= 2,097,152 = 2^21`. Since `log2` of a doubled number rises by exactly 1, every time the data doubles you pay a single extra step — 4M → 22, 8M → 23, and so on. This is the whole intuition behind 'logarithmic': a linear scan would need a *million* more comparisons for that same doubling, while binary search needs one. Doubling the data barely moves the cost.
